data.title: Shell Weighs North West Shelf Exit And Future LNG Cash Flows
data.description:
• Shell is reportedly exploring a sale of its stake in the North West Shelf LNG project, Australia's largest LNG facility.
  • Interest is said to be coming from Abu Dhabi's XRG fund and Japanese consortia, which are preparing potential bids.
  • The discussions focus on a core LNG asset that has long been important to regional energy flows and global gas markets.

For investors watching LSE:SHEL, this potential divestment sits against a backdrop where the shares last closed at £29.44. Over the past year the stock has returned 14.1%, with gains of 6.7% year to date and 8.2% over the past month, while the 5 year return stands at 139.9%. These figures show how meaningful any change in Shell's LNG portfolio could be for sentiment around the stock.

The possible sale could reshape Shell's exposure to Australian LNG and alter its mix of cash flow sources across regions. Investors may focus on how any transaction price, structure and reinvestment plans affect Shell's overall portfolio and capital allocation priorities. The level of interest from state backed and Japanese buyers also highlights demand for established LNG capacity in global energy supply chains.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Shell by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Shell.

We've flagged 1 risk for Shell. See which could impact your investment.

For Shell, a sale of its 16.67% stake in the North West Shelf LNG project would sit squarely in its ongoing portfolio high-grading, trimming exposure to more mature assets while keeping focus on LNG growth elsewhere. With XRG and Japanese groups reportedly circling an interest valued around US$2b, the key question for you as an investor is how any proceeds would be used, especially given Shell's active dividend and buyback program in recent quarters. The North West Shelf is a long-standing, infrastructure-heavy asset in a region where Woodside now controls 50%, so Shell may see limited operating influence relative to its global LNG options in Canada, Egypt or Trinidad & Tobago.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Shell Narrative

  • The potential sale lines up with the narrative of high-grading the portfolio, recycling capital from legacy LNG infrastructure into areas where Shell sees better long-term returns and trading flexibility.
  • At the same time, stepping back from a critical Australian LNG hub could challenge the idea that Shell wants to maintain broad LNG optionality across all key basins.
  • The reported competitive tension between XRG and Japanese consortia, and any valuation gap versus the Chevron sale to Woodside in 2024, is not fully reflected in the existing narrative, which focuses more on the overall LNG growth plan than asset-specific pricing.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Shell to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Selling down in Australia could concentrate LNG exposure elsewhere, which may increase reliance on a smaller set of projects if contract or regulatory conditions change.
  • ⚠️ The North West Shelf is seen as important for Western Australia's domestic gas system, so regulatory and approval risk around any buyer, particularly state-backed or foreign groups, could complicate timing and terms.
  • 🎁 A clean exit at a solid price could free up around US$2b that Shell might allocate to higher-return LNG projects, its ongoing share buybacks, or balance-sheet priorities.
  • 🎁 Interest from multiple bidders suggests Shell holds a premium, long-life LNG asset that could crystallise value relative to keeping the stake on its books.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to watch three things. First, any confirmation of a formal sale process, including whether Shell discloses a target valuation range or preferred buyer profile. Second, how management talks about redeploying potential proceeds, especially given recent share buybacks and dividend payments. Third, what this means for Shell's LNG footprint compared with peers such as BP and TotalEnergies, which are also active in global gas and liquefied natural gas. The eventual buyer mix and regulatory reaction in Australia will give you more clues on execution risk and timing.

data.title: BroadBand Security's (TSE:4398) Soft Earnings Are Actually Better Than They Appear
data.description:
Shareholders appeared unconcerned with BroadBand Security, Inc.'s (TSE:4398) lackluster earnings report last week. We did some digging, and we believe the earnings are stronger than they seem.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

Zooming In On BroadBand Security's Earnings

One key financial ratio used to measure how well a company converts its profit to free cash flow (FCF) is the accrual ratio. To get the accrual ratio we first subtract FCF from profit for a period, and then divide that number by the average operating assets for the period. You could think of the accrual ratio from cashflow as the 'non-FCF profit ratio'.

Therefore, it's actually considered a good thing when a company has a negative accrual ratio, but a bad thing if its accrual ratio is positive. While having an accrual ratio above zero is of little concern, we do think it's worth noting when a company has a relatively high accrual ratio. That's because some academic studies have suggested that high accruals ratios tend to lead to lower profit or less profit growth.

BroadBand Security has an accrual ratio of -0.10 for the year to December 2025. That implies it has good cash conversion, and implies that its free cash flow solidly exceeded its profit last year. In fact, it had free cash flow of JP¥282m in the last year, which was a lot more than its statutory profit of JP¥186.0m. BroadBand Security did see its free cash flow drop year on year, which is less than ideal, like a Simpson's episode without Groundskeeper Willie.

Note: we always recommend investors check balance sheet strength. Click here to be taken to our balance sheet analysis of BroadBand Security.

Our Take On BroadBand Security's Profit Performance

BroadBand Security's accrual ratio is solid, and indicates strong free cash flow, as we discussed, above. Because of this, we think BroadBand Security's earnings potential is at least as good as it seems, and maybe even better! Unfortunately, though, its earnings per share actually fell back over the last year. data.title: Howmet Aerospace Weighs CAM Acquisition Against Higher Debt And Growth Aims
data.description:
• Howmet Aerospace (NYSE:HWM) announced a multi billion dollar plan to acquire Consolidated Aerospace Manufacturing, CAM.
  • The deal will be financed through a major public offering of senior notes, representing a significant new debt commitment.
  • The transaction is positioned as a material expansion of Howmet's aerospace components footprint and customer reach.

For shareholders watching NYSE:HWM, this acquisition comes with the stock trading at $249.35 and a very large 3 year return, alongside a roughly 7x 5 year return. Recent price moves, up 8.0% over the past week, 10.9% over the past month, 17.8% year to date, and 79.3% over the past year, frame this announcement as a key development for an already strongly performing name.

The planned CAM acquisition and related senior notes offering could reshape Howmet's business mix and capital structure. The focus now is on deal terms, integration plans, and debt service capacity. As more details emerge, investors can watch how the market prices in the added scale, new revenue streams, and the higher leverage that comes with a multi billion dollar transaction.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Howmet Aerospace by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Howmet Aerospace.

📰 Beyond the headline: 1 risk and 2 things going right for Howmet Aerospace that every investor should see.

The CAM deal marks a sizeable step-up in scale for Howmet, with an approximately US$1.8b purchase price funded mainly through new fixed-rate senior notes and additional borrowings. The three tranches already priced, at coupons of 3.75% due 2028, 3.90% due 2029, and 4.75% due 2036, lock in funding costs for the long term and sit as senior, unsecured obligations. For you as a shareholder, the key question is whether CAM’s fasteners and related aerospace hardware can support earnings enough to justify the higher interest burden and leverage. Management has recently been buying back shares and reporting higher sales and EPS, so this deal pivots capital allocation toward expansion rather than returning cash. If CAM broadens content per aircraft and deepens ties with major customers also served by peers like RTX, TransDigm, and Honeywell, it could reinforce Howmet’s role across key aerospace platforms. The trade off is a more debt-heavy balance sheet just as guidance points to higher revenue in 2026, so your focus now is on management’s integration discipline, synergy delivery, and any updates to capital return plans once the acquisition closes.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Howmet Aerospace Narrative

  • The CAM acquisition aligns with the narrative around higher content per aircraft, particularly in fasteners and structural components, which are central to long term aero demand and margin potential.
  • Heavier use of debt funding could challenge the narrative’s focus on strong free cash flow, as higher interest expense may offset some of the margin gains analysts expect from operational improvements.
  • The narrative highlights capacity expansions and automation, but it does not fully account for execution risk and cost overlap from integrating a large acquired business like CAM.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Howmet Aerospace to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Higher leverage from US$1.8b of acquisition funding increases sensitivity to any slowdown in aerospace demand or integration setbacks at CAM.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged a high level of debt as a risk, and this new note issuance adds to those concerns if cash generation does not track expectations.
  • 🎁 The acquisition could deepen Howmet’s role on key aircraft platforms, supporting revenue growth alongside the 2026 revenue guidance range of US$9b to US$9.2b.
  • 🎁 Earnings momentum, with full year 2025 EPS above the prior year and guidance in place, gives the company more financial flexibility as it absorbs CAM.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on three things. First, any updated leverage and interest expense targets once the senior notes offering is fully closed and CAM’s numbers are folded in. Second, management’s commentary on synergies, especially around cross selling, procurement, and manufacturing efficiency, and how quickly those show up in margins. Third, whether capital returns such as buybacks slow after the deal, or if strong cash generation allows Howmet to resume them while reducing debt. The reaction of large institutional holders, as well as how competitors like RTX and TransDigm talk about fastener and hardware demand, will also help you gauge whether this acquisition strengthens Howmet’s position in the aero supply chain.

data.title: Solocal Group (ENXTPA:LOCAL) Net Margin Collapse Challenges Bullish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Solocal Group (ENXTPA:LOCAL) has just posted its FY 2025 numbers, with second half revenue of €160.4 million and net income of €8.8 million, while the trailing twelve months show revenue of €324.5 million and net income of €14.2 million anchoring the earnings picture. The business has seen half year revenue move from €170.7 million in the second half of 2024 to €164.1 million in the first half of 2025 and then to €160.4 million in the latest period, alongside EPS of €5.47, €5.07 and €0.16 across those same snapshots. With that backdrop, the key question for investors now is how sustainable the current profit profile is given the pressure you can see building in margins.

See our full analysis for Solocal Group.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to test them against the widely held narratives around Solocal Group, highlighting where the latest earnings reinforce the story and where they start to push back against it.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Net margin drops from 35.9% to 4.4%

  • Over the trailing twelve months, Solocal Group booked €324.5 million in revenue and €14.2 million in net income, which works out to a 4.4% net margin versus 35.9% a year earlier, so profit now represents a much smaller slice of each euro of sales than it did previously.
  • What stands out for a cautious, bearish take is how this margin line sits next to the earnings growth stats, with five year earnings growth of 18.6% per year and forecasts around 22% per year looking strong on paper. Yet the recent 4.4% margin compared with 35.9% a year earlier suggests a profitability profile that is much thinner than the growth rates alone might imply.
    • Bears can point to this compression as a sign that even with €324.5 million of trailing revenue, the company is currently keeping far less profit in absolute terms than when margins were above 30%.
    • At the same time, the combination of strong multi year earnings growth figures with a sharply lower margin gives you a mixed picture, where headline growth and current profitability quality are pulling in different directions.

Debt and negative equity sit behind the story

  • The risk summary flags that Solocal Group is operating with negative shareholders’ equity and a high level of debt, so in addition to the 4.4% net margin, the balance sheet itself is a key part of the risk profile around those earnings.
  • Critics highlight that this capital structure can weigh heavily on how investors view the recent profit numbers, because the same €14.2 million of trailing net income has to be considered alongside obligations to lenders and an equity base that is reported as negative.
    • This combination means that even if earnings have grown 18.6% per year over five years, there is less of a cushion from equity if business conditions turn, compared with a company that reports positive net assets.
    • The high debt level also means more of the cash generated from the €324.5 million of revenue may need to support financing costs rather than being available for reinvestment or returns to shareholders.

Low 11.3x P/E against peers and DCF fair value

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 11.3x at a price of €4.49, compared with a 47.9x peer average and 17.6x for the Global Interactive Media and Services industry, while a DCF fair value of €14.86 is well above the current price in the dataset.
  • What is interesting for a more optimistic, bullish angle is that this combination of a lower P/E multiple and a DCF fair value above the market price sits next to earnings growth of 18.6% per year over five years and a forecast around 22% per year. Some investors may read this as a gap between how the business has been performing on earnings metrics and how it is currently valued.
    • Supporters of the bullish case can point to the 11.3x multiple versus 47.9x peers as evidence that the stock is priced more cautiously than many companies in the same space despite those growth figures.
    • At the same time, the presence of negative shareholders’ equity and a 4.4% trailing net margin gives a clear explanation for why the market might hesitate to close that gap with the €14.86 DCF fair value, keeping both upside and risk in view.

Some investors look at that 11.3x P/E, the €14.86 DCF fair value, and the forecast earnings growth and want to see how the full bull and bear arguments stack up side by side before deciding what it all means for them personally, which is where a structured narrative can really help join the dots between the raw numbers and the wider story around Solocal Group. Examining Cashflow Against Visual Processing Japan's Earnings

Many investors haven't heard of the accrual ratio from cashflow, but it is actually a useful measure of how well a company's profit is backed up by free cash flow (FCF) during a given period. To get the accrual ratio we first subtract FCF from profit for a period, and then divide that number by the average operating assets for the period. You could think of the accrual ratio from cashflow as the 'non-FCF profit ratio'.

Therefore, it's actually considered a good thing when a company has a negative accrual ratio, but a bad thing if its accrual ratio is positive. While it's not a problem to have a positive accrual ratio, indicating a certain level of non-cash profits, a high accrual ratio is arguably a bad thing, because it indicates paper profits are not matched by cash flow. That's because some academic studies have suggested that high accruals ratios tend to lead to lower profit or less profit growth.

Over the twelve months to December 2025, Visual Processing Japan recorded an accrual ratio of -0.66. Therefore, its statutory earnings were very significantly less than its free cashflow. In fact, it had free cash flow of JP¥228m in the last year, which was a lot more than its statutory profit of JP¥176.0m. Visual Processing Japan shareholders are no doubt pleased that free cash flow improved over the last twelve months.

And on top of that, its earnings per share have grown at an extremely impressive rate over the last three years. data.title: Arent (TSE:5254) Is Posting Healthy Earnings, But It Is Not All Good News
data.description:
Despite posting strong earnings, Arent Inc.'s (TSE:5254) stock didn't move much over the last week. We looked deeper into the numbers and found that shareholders might be concerned with some underlying weaknesses.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

A Closer Look At Arent's Earnings

In high finance, the key ratio used to measure how well a company converts reported profits into free cash flow (FCF) is the accrual ratio (from cashflow). The accrual ratio subtracts the FCF from the profit for a given period, and divides the result by the average operating assets of the company over that time. You could think of the accrual ratio from cashflow as the 'non-FCF profit ratio'.

As a result, a negative accrual ratio is a positive for the company, and a positive accrual ratio is a negative. While it's not a problem to have a positive accrual ratio, indicating a certain level of non-cash profits, a high accrual ratio is arguably a bad thing, because it indicates paper profits are not matched by cash flow. That's because some academic studies have suggested that high accruals ratios tend to lead to lower profit or less profit growth.

Over the twelve months to December 2025, Arent recorded an accrual ratio of 0.45. That means it didn't generate anywhere near enough free cash flow to match its profit. Statistically speaking, that's a real negative for future earnings. To wit, it produced free cash flow of JP¥333m during the period, falling well short of its reported profit of JP¥1.33b. Arent shareholders will no doubt be hoping that its free cash flow bounces back next year, since it was down over the last twelve months. However, that's not the end of the story. We can look at how unusual items in the profit and loss statement impacted its accrual ratio, as well as explore how dilution is impacting shareholders negatively. The good news for shareholders is that Arent's accrual ratio was much better last year, so this year's poor reading might simply be a case of a short term mismatch between profit and FCF. As a result, some shareholders may be looking for stronger cash conversion in the current year.

See our latest analysis for Arent

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

One essential aspect of assessing earnings quality is to look at how much a company is diluting shareholders. Arent expanded the number of shares on issue by 12% over the last year. Therefore, each share now receives a smaller portion of profit. To celebrate net income while ignoring dilution is like rejoicing because you have a single slice of a larger pizza, but ignoring the fact that the pizza is now cut into many more slices. Check out Arent's historical EPS growth by clicking on this link.

A Look At The Impact Of Arent's Dilution On Its Earnings Per Share (EPS)

Arent has improved its profit over the last three years, with an annualized gain of 879% in that time. In comparison, earnings per share only gained 751% over the same period. And the 61% profit boost in the last year certainly seems impressive at first glance. But in comparison, EPS only increased by 54% over the same period. And so, you can see quite clearly that dilution is influencing shareholder earnings.

Changes in the share price do tend to reflect changes in earnings per share, in the long run. So Arent shareholders will want to see that EPS figure continue to increase. However, if its profit increases while its earnings per share stay flat (or even fall) then shareholders might not see much benefit. For the ordinary retail shareholder, EPS is a great measure to check your hypothetical "share" of the company's profit.

The Impact Of Unusual Items On Profit

Given the accrual ratio, it's not overly surprising that Arent's profit was boosted by unusual items worth JP¥220m in the last twelve months. While we like to see profit increases, we tend to be a little more cautious when unusual items have made a big contribution. We ran the numbers on most publicly listed companies worldwide, and it's very common for unusual items to be once-off in nature. And that's as you'd expect, given these boosts are described as 'unusual'. Assuming those unusual items don't show up again in the current year, we'd thus expect profit to be weaker next year (in the absence of business growth, that is).

Our Take On Arent's Profit Performance

In conclusion, Arent's weak accrual ratio suggested its statutory earnings have been inflated by the unusual items. Meanwhile, the new shares issued mean that shareholders now own less of the company, unless they tipped in more cash themselves. For all the reasons mentioned above, we think that, at a glance, Arent's statutory profits could be considered to be low quality, because they are likely to give investors an overly positive impression of the company. So while earnings quality is important, it's equally important to consider the risks facing Arent at this point in time. Case in point: We've spotted 2 warning signs for Arent you should be mindful of and 1 of them is concerning.

Our examination of Arent has focussed on certain factors that can make its earnings look better than they are. And, on that basis, we are somewhat skeptical. Zooming In On SIIX's Earnings

Many investors haven't heard of the accrual ratio from cashflow, but it is actually a useful measure of how well a company's profit is backed up by free cash flow (FCF) during a given period. To get the accrual ratio we first subtract FCF from profit for a period, and then divide that number by the average operating assets for the period. This ratio tells us how much of a company's profit is not backed by free cashflow.

That means a negative accrual ratio is a good thing, because it shows that the company is bringing in more free cash flow than its profit would suggest. While it's not a problem to have a positive accrual ratio, indicating a certain level of non-cash profits, a high accrual ratio is arguably a bad thing, because it indicates paper profits are not matched by cash flow. SIIX's free cash flow improved over the last year, which is generally good to see. However, that's not all there is to consider. data.title: We Like The Quality Of Asia Enterprises Holding's (SGX:A55) Earnings
data.description:
Asia Enterprises Holding Limited's (SGX:A55) solid earnings announcement recently didn't do much to the stock price. We did some digging, and we think that investors are missing some encouraging factors in the underlying numbers.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

A Closer Look At Asia Enterprises Holding's Earnings

In high finance, the key ratio used to measure how well a company converts reported profits into free cash flow (FCF) is the accrual ratio (from cashflow). The accrual ratio subtracts the FCF from the profit for a given period, and divides the result by the average operating assets of the company over that time. You could think of the accrual ratio from cashflow as the 'non-FCF profit ratio'.

As a result, a negative accrual ratio is a positive for the company, and a positive accrual ratio is a negative. While it's not a problem to have a positive accrual ratio, indicating a certain level of non-cash profits, a high accrual ratio is arguably a bad thing, because it indicates paper profits are not matched by cash flow. Notably, there is some academic evidence that suggests that a high accrual ratio is a bad sign for near-term profits, generally speaking.

For the year to December 2025, Asia Enterprises Holding had an accrual ratio of -0.17. That implies it has very good cash conversion, and that its earnings in the last year actually significantly understate its free cash flow. To wit, it produced free cash flow of S$8.2m during the period, dwarfing its reported profit of S$1.30m. Asia Enterprises Holding's free cash flow actually declined over the last year, which is disappointing, like non-biodegradable balloons. Unfortunately for shareholders, the company has also been issuing new shares, diluting their share of future earnings.

Note: we always recommend investors check balance sheet strength. Click here to be taken to our balance sheet analysis of Asia Enterprises Holding.

One essential aspect of assessing earnings quality is to look at how much a company is diluting shareholders. As it happens, Asia Enterprises Holding issued 8.3% more new shares over the last year. Therefore, each share now receives a smaller portion of profit. Per share metrics like EPS help us understand how much actual shareholders are benefitting from the company's profits, while the net income level gives us a better view of the company's absolute size. Check out Asia Enterprises Holding's historical EPS growth by clicking on this link.

A Look At The Impact Of Asia Enterprises Holding's Dilution On Its Earnings Per Share (EPS)

Unfortunately, Asia Enterprises Holding's profit is down 65% per year over three years. On the bright side, in the last twelve months it grew profit by 251%. But EPS was less impressive, up only 224% in that time. And so, you can see quite clearly that dilution is influencing shareholder earnings.

Changes in the share price do tend to reflect changes in earnings per share, in the long run. So it will certainly be a positive for shareholders if Asia Enterprises Holding can grow EPS persistently. However, if its profit increases while its earnings per share stay flat (or even fall) then shareholders might not see much benefit. For that reason, you could say that EPS is more important that net income in the long run, assuming the goal is to assess whether a company's share price might grow.

Our Take On Asia Enterprises Holding's Profit Performance

In conclusion, Asia Enterprises Holding has strong cashflow relative to earnings, which indicates good quality earnings, but the dilution means its earnings per share growth is weaker than its profit growth. Based on these factors, we think that Asia Enterprises Holding's profits are a reasonably conservative guide to its underlying profitability. In light of this, if you'd like to do more analysis on the company, it's vital to be informed of the risks involved. Every company has risks, and we've spotted 4 warning signs for Asia Enterprises Holding (of which 2 are concerning!) you should know about.

In this article we've looked at a number of factors that can impair the utility of profit numbers, as a guide to a business. But there are plenty of other ways to inform your opinion of a company. data.title: Income Investors Should Know That Westports Holdings Berhad (KLSE:WPRTS) Goes Ex-Dividend Soon
data.description:
Regular readers will know that we love our dividends at Simply Wall St, which is why it's exciting to see Westports Holdings Berhad (KLSE:WPRTS) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next four days. The ex-dividend date is two business days before a company's record date in most cases, which is the date on which the company determines which shareholders are entitled to receive a dividend. It is important to be aware of the ex-dividend date because any trade on the stock needs to have been settled on or before the record date. Therefore, if you purchase Westports Holdings Berhad's shares on or after the 24th of February, you won't be eligible to receive the dividend, when it is paid on the 25th of March.

The company's next dividend payment will be RM00.1192 per share. Last year, in total, the company distributed RM0.22 to shareholders. Calculating the last year's worth of payments shows that Westports Holdings Berhad has a trailing yield of 3.5% on the current share price of RM06.24. Dividends are a major contributor to investment returns for long term holders, but only if the dividend continues to be paid. As a result, readers should always check whether Westports Holdings Berhad has been able to grow its dividends, or if the dividend might be cut.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Dividends are usually paid out of company profits, so if a company pays out more than it earned then its dividend is usually at greater risk of being cut. Westports Holdings Berhad paid out more than half (75%) of its earnings last year, which is a regular payout ratio for most companies. Yet cash flows are even more important than profits for assessing a dividend, so we need to see if the company generated enough cash to pay its distribution. It paid out more than half (69%) of its free cash flow in the past year, which is within an average range for most companies.

It's encouraging to see that the dividend is covered by both profit and cash flow. This generally suggests the dividend is sustainable, as long as earnings don't drop precipitously.

Companies with consistently growing earnings per share generally make the best dividend stocks, as they usually find it easier to grow dividends per share. If business enters a downturn and the dividend is cut, the company could see its value fall precipitously. This is why it's a relief to see Westports Holdings Berhad earnings per share are up 8.7% per annum over the last five years. While earnings have been growing at a credible rate, the company is paying out a majority of its earnings to shareholders. If management lifts the payout ratio further, we'd take this as a tacit signal that the company's growth prospects are slowing.

Many investors will assess a company's dividend performance by evaluating how much the dividend payments have changed over time. Since the start of our data, 10 years ago, Westports Holdings Berhad has lifted its dividend by approximately 6.9% a year on average. We're glad to see dividends rising alongside earnings over a number of years, which may be a sign the company intends to share the growth with shareholders.

To Sum It Up

Should investors buy Westports Holdings Berhad for the upcoming dividend? Earnings per share have been growing modestly and Westports Holdings Berhad paid out a bit over half of its earnings and free cash flow last year. It might be worth researching if the company is reinvesting in growth projects that could grow earnings and dividends in the future, but for now we're not all that optimistic on its dividend prospects.
